subject: Physics
options: helical path with the axis perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>
helical path with the axis along magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>
straight along the direction of magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>
solution:
The formula to calculate the radius of the path followed by the charged particle under an external magnetic field can be written as

r=mvBq   ...1

The perpendicular component of velocity results in circular motion, while the parallel component results in linear motion. Hence, the path of the charged particle is helical in nature, the axis of which lies along the direction of the magnetic field.

subject: Physics
solution:
The Lorentz force is given by F→=q(v→×B→)=qvBsinθn^. The angle θ=0°. Hence the force experienced is zero.

Thus, the electron will not experience any magnetic force and the electron will continue to move along the axis.

solution:
When a charged particle enters the magnetic field in perpendicular direction then it experience a force in perpendicular direction.
i.e. $F=B q v \sin \theta$
Due to which it moves in a circular path.

subject: Physics
solution:
Net force on proton inside an electric and a magnetic field is given by

F→e+F→m=qE→+qv→×B→    ...1

If both E→ and B→ are zero, then F→e and F→m both are zero. Hence, velocity may remain constant. Therefore, option (A) is correct.

If E=0, B≠0, but velocity is parallel or antiparallel to the magnetic field, then also F→e and F→m both are zero. Hence, option (B) is also correct.

If E≠0, B≠0, but F→e + F→m=0, then also velocity may remain constant. Thus, option (D) is also correct.

subject: Physics
solution:
The magnetic force on a moving charged particle is given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

Since the acceleration $\vec{a}$ is in the direction of the magnetic force, it must be perpendicular to the magnetic field $\vec{B}$.

Therefore, the dot product of acceleration and magnetic field is zero:

$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{B} = 0$

Substituting the given vectors:

$\left(4\hat{i} - \dfrac{x}{2}\hat{j}\right) \cdot (3\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}) = 0$

$(4)(3) + \left(-\dfrac{x}{2}\right)(2) = 0$

$12 - x = 0$

$x = 12$

Answer: $12$

Force on conductor
F→=I(l→×b→)

F → =-10( 3 a ^ z )×( 3× 10 −4 e −0.2x a ^ y )

⇒F→=90×10-4(e-0.2x) along with x-axis

work done on the conductor in moving along x-axis.

W=∫x=02F→.dx→

W = ∫ 0 2 Bi ℓ dx = ∫ 0 2 3 × 10 - 4 e - 0.2x × 3 × 10dx =90×10-4e-0.2x-0.202

= 450 × 10 - 4 [ 1 - e - 0.4 ] = 180 × 10 - 4 ( Using e - x = 1 - x )

P = W Δ t = 180 × 10 - 4 5 × 10 - 3 = 3.6 W.

Hence, closest option is 2.97 W.
(if we used exact value of exponential term will get exact answer).

$\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}= & {\left[\frac{-\mu_0(2 \mathrm{I} / 3)}{4 \pi(\mathrm{a} / 2)} \sqrt{2}-\frac{\mu_0(2 \mathrm{I} / 3)}{4 \pi(\mathrm{a} / 2)} \sqrt{2}\right.} \\ & \left.+\frac{\mu_0(\mathrm{I} / 3)}{4 \pi(\mathrm{a} / 2)} \sqrt{2}+\frac{\mu_0(\mathrm{I} / 3)}{4 \pi(\mathrm{a} / 2)} \sqrt{2}\right] \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}= & {\left[\frac{-2 \sqrt{2} \mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{3 \pi \mathrm{a}}+\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{3 \pi \mathrm{a}}\right] \hat{\mathrm{k}} } \\ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}= & \frac{-\sqrt{2} \mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{3 \pi \mathrm{a}} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
As $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ is $\perp$ to $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$, so charge particle will move in circular path, whose radius is given by
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}}$

Starting point $\rightarrow \mathrm{A}$
Ending point $\rightarrow \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore$ Net displacement $=\mathrm{AC}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{CD}-\mathrm{AD} \\ & \mathrm{AC}=\frac{2 \mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}_1}-\frac{2 \mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}_2} \\ & \mathrm{AC}=\frac{2 \mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}_1}\left[1-\frac{\mathrm{B}_1}{\mathrm{~B}_2}\right]\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
The magnetic dipole moment of the current carrying loop is given by $\vec{M} = I A \hat{n}$.

Given radius $r = 2$ cm $= 2 \times 10^{-2}$ m, the area of the loop is:
$A = \pi r^2 = \pi (2 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 4\pi \times 10^{-4}$ m$^2$

Substituting the given values $I = 100\sqrt{2}$ A and $\hat{n} = \dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{2}}$:
$\vec{M} = (100\sqrt{2}) \times (4\pi \times 10^{-4}) \times \left(\dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$
$\vec{M} = 4\pi \times 10^{-2} (\hat{i}+\hat{k})$ A$\cdot$m$^2$

The torque experienced by the loop in the magnetic field is $\vec{\tau} = \vec{M} \times \vec{B}$.
Given $\vec{B} = 4 \times 10^{-3} (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})$ T, we have:
$\vec{\tau} = [4\pi \times 10^{-2} (\hat{i}+\hat{k})] \times [4 \times 10^{-3} (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})]$
$\vec{\tau} = 16\pi \times 10^{-5} [(\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})]$

Evaluating the cross product:
$(\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k}) = (\hat{i} \times 3\hat{i}) + (\hat{i} \times 2\hat{k}) + (\hat{k} \times 3\hat{i}) + (\hat{k} \times 2\hat{k})$
$= 0 + 2(-\hat{j}) + 3(\hat{j}) + 0 = \hat{j}$

Therefore, the torque is:
$\vec{\tau} = 16\pi \times 10^{-5} \hat{j}$

Substituting $\pi = 3.14$:
$\vec{\tau} = 16 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-5} \hat{j} = 50.24 \times 10^{-5} \hat{j} = 5024 \times 10^{-7} \hat{j}$ Wb$\cdot$A

subject: Physics

Putting the values, 

ϕ=400×μrμ0nI×A

⇒4π×10-6 =400μr×4π×10-7×4000.4×0.4×2×10-4

⇒μr=125

subject: Physics
solution:
Force between two parallel current carrying wires is given as

f=μ0I1I22πdl

F∝I1I2

When I1, I2 are in same direction the I1I2 is+ve ⇒ attraction ⇒ -ve

When I1I2 are in opposite direction then

I1I2=-ve

∴ Force = repulsive = +ve

subject: Physics
solution:
Given,

Current in the arc A is i1=2 A

Current in the arc B is i2= 3 A

Radius of arc A is R1=2 cm

Radius of arc B is R2=4 cm

Magnetic field at the centre of circular current carrying arc is given by

BC=μ0i4πRθ  ...(1) 

Where, θ is the angle subtended at centre.

Magnetic field at the centre due to arc A and B is given by 

BA=μ0i14πR12π-π2  ...2

BB=μ0i24πR22π-π3  ...3

Dividing equation (2) by (1), we get

⇒BABB=i1i2×R22π−π2R12π−π3

⇒BABB=23×42×3π2×35π

⇒BABB=65
